questions.question_text: Under which license type must a Missouri salesperson operate?
distractors: Independently with MREC approval
Under the Division of Professional Registration
Under any broker-salesperson
questions.explanation: Missouri salespersons must always work under the supervision of a licensed broker. They cannot operate independently or hold their own escrow accounts. A broker-salesperson also works under a broker and cannot have salespersons licensed under him or her, though one may manage a branch office for the broker (20 CSR 2250-4.050, 8.030).

questions.question_text: A licensee holds a broker license but works under another broker rather than operating independently. What is this person's license classification?
distractors: Associate broker
Managing broker
Inactive broker
questions.explanation: In Missouri, a person who holds a broker license but chooses to work under another broker is classified as a broker-salesperson. This person has broker qualifications but functions similarly to a salesperson under the supervising broker. They cannot simultaneously operate an independent brokerage.

questions.question_text: Which license type authorizes a Missouri licensee to maintain escrow accounts and supervise salespersons?
distractors: Salesperson
Broker-salesperson
Managing associate
questions.explanation: Only a broker operating independently is authorized to maintain escrow (trust) accounts and supervise salespersons and broker-salespersons. A broker-salesperson, despite holding a broker license, works under another broker and does not have independent escrow or supervisory authority.

questions.question_text: A broker-salesperson wants to open her own brokerage while continuing to work under her current broker. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: No, she cannot operate both simultaneously
distractors: Yes, with MREC written approval
Yes, if both brokers consent in writing
Yes, but only in different counties
questions.explanation: Missouri does not allow a broker-salesperson to operate an independent brokerage while simultaneously working under another broker. She must choose one arrangement. To open her own firm, she must terminate her broker-salesperson status and activate as an independent broker.

questions.question_text: A newly licensed salesperson asks what distinguishes a broker from a broker-salesperson in daily practice. Which answer is most accurate?
questions.correct_answer: A broker operates independently; a B-S does not
distractors: A broker earns higher commissions by law
A B-S can supervise up to 5 salespersons
There is no practical difference in duties
questions.explanation: The key distinction is operational independence. A broker can operate a firm, maintain escrow accounts, and supervise licensees. A broker-salesperson holds broker credentials but works under another broker without independent authority. Commission rates are market-driven, not set by law.

questions.question_text: What is the renewal fee for a Missouri salesperson license?
questions.explanation: Effective July 1, 2024, 20 CSR 2250-5.020 sets the salesperson renewal fee at $65 for the two-year license period; the broker and broker-salesperson renewal fee is $75. The salesperson fee was about $40 before July 2024, but that figure is outdated. Late renewals add a delinquent fee of $50 per month or partial month, capped at $200.

questions.question_text: A Missouri broker renews 3 months after the June 30 deadline. What total late penalty applies?
questions.explanation: Missouri charges a $50 delinquent fee per month or partial month for overdue renewals; fees are not prorated. At 3 months late, the penalty is $150 (3 x $50). The maximum late penalty cap is $200, so this broker has not yet reached the maximum. The penalty is added to the standard renewal fee.

questions.question_text: A salesperson renewed 6 months late. What is the maximum late penalty charged?
questions.explanation: Missouri caps late renewal penalties at $200 regardless of how many months the renewal is overdue. Even at 6 months late ($50 x 6 = $300), the licensee only pays the $200 maximum penalty. This cap prevents penalties from becoming prohibitive for licensees who eventually renew.

questions.question_text: What is the renewal fee for a Missouri broker license?
questions.explanation: Effective July 1, 2024, 20 CSR 2250-5.020 sets the renewal fee for broker, broker-salesperson, and entity licenses at $75, higher than the $65 salesperson renewal fee. The $50 figure is the pre-July 2024 amount, and $100 is the original issuance fee for a broker license (paid together with a separate $50 application fee on original application), not the renewal fee.

questions.question_text: A broker renewed on time but a salesperson in the same office renewed 4 months late. The broker claims the salesperson should pay no penalty because the broker renewed on time. Is this correct?
distractors: Yes, the broker's renewal covers the office
Yes, if the broker pays the penalty instead
No, but the broker shares the penalty equally
questions.explanation: Each licensee is individually responsible for their own renewal and associated penalties. A broker's timely renewal has no effect on a salesperson's separate obligation. The salesperson owes $200 (4 months x $50, hitting the $200 cap) regardless of the broker's status.

questions.question_text: A Missouri broker opens a second office location. What must the broker do regarding MREC?
questions.correct_answer: Register the branch office with MREC
distractors: Obtain a separate broker license
Hire a co-broker for the branch
No action is required for branches
questions.explanation: Missouri requires each branch office to be registered with MREC -- the broker must notify the commission in writing within 10 days of opening or changing the address or managing licensee of a branch office (20 CSR 2250-8.030). The broker does not need a separate license for each branch; every branch operates under the same name and license as the parent office.

questions.question_text: A salesperson wants to transfer from Broker A to Broker B. What must occur for this transfer?
questions.correct_answer: The license must be transferred through MREC
distractors: The salesperson must retake the state exam
Broker A must approve the transfer in writing
A 30-day waiting period is always required
questions.explanation: When a salesperson changes brokers in Missouri, the license transfer must be processed through MREC. The salesperson does not need to retake the exam. While Broker A should be notified, their approval is not required to transfer. The new broker (Broker B) initiates the transfer process.

questions.question_text: A broker is closing her brokerage permanently. Which action must she take regarding her affiliated licensees?
questions.correct_answer: Notify them in writing and return their licenses to MREC
distractors: Guarantee every licensee signs with a new broker before closing
Return all licenses to the licensees directly
Request MREC to suspend all affiliated licenses
questions.explanation: Under 20 CSR 2250-8.155(1)(A)2, a broker voluntarily closing a firm must notify all affiliated licensees in writing of the effective closing date and return their licenses to the commission with the closing statement. The broker must also notify MREC on its prescribed form, notify clients, remove advertising, maintain escrow accounts until funds are disbursed, and arrange for pending contracts to be closed. Each licensee then has six months to transfer to a new broker or change license status; the closing broker is not required to guarantee transfers.

questions.question_text: A broker operates 3 branch offices. How many of these branches must be registered with MREC?
distractors: Only the largest branch
Only branches in different counties
None if the main office is registered
questions.explanation: Missouri requires every branch office to be registered with MREC: the broker must notify the commission in writing within 10 days of opening each one, and each branch operates under the same name and license as the parent office (20 CSR 2250-8.030). The only offices excused from branch registration are on-site project sales, leasing, or management offices at a development, apartment complex, or similar property -- a category that does not apply to ordinary branch offices.

questions.question_text: A salesperson transferring to a new broker mails a properly completed transfer application to MREC by certified mail, with all required materials in one envelope. When may she begin practicing under the new broker?
questions.correct_answer: Immediately - she is deemed transferred upon mailing
distractors: Only after MREC issues the new license
After a mandatory 10-day MREC review period
Only after her former broker consents in writing
questions.explanation: Under 20 CSR 2250-4.050(4), a salesperson or broker-salesperson transferring between brokers without changing license type is deemed transferred at the time a properly completed application is mailed to MREC by certified, registered, or overnight delivery with all required materials under one cover. The new broker is responsible for the application's completeness and mailing. If the application is incomplete, the transfer is not effective until the new license is received or MREC confirms in writing that it is being processed -- and original licensees or licensees changing license type or status must wait for that confirmation before practicing.
